serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion is one can or bottle of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, commonly consumed as a chilled ready-to-drink beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit juice drink with aloe vera — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Fruit juice drink with aloe vera' (cr_id D03070007). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fruit juice and fruit-based beverages — analogous estimate based on fruit juice drink category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific commercial fruit juice drink with aloe vera. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to fruit juice drinks and diluted fruit beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 45–65 for similar sugar-containing fruit beverages. A GI of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55) and is classified as low per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 20.45) / 100 ≈ 11.2 for a 250 g serving (20.45 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.99 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 8.99 g/100 g sugar content far exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and liquid sugar intake is independently associated with elevated blood pressure regardless of the low sodium (12 mg/100 g) content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.99 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.02 g/100 g); liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose excursions and is contraindicated in type 2 diabetes management per ADA Standards of Care.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this drink; the high free-sugar load (8.99 g/100 g) with virtually no fibre produces rapid blood glucose spikes, and the aloe vera content at commercial concentrations is insufficient to offset this glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk; this drink contains 8.99 g sugar/100 g with no protective nutrients such as fibre, omega-3, or antioxidants at meaningful levels.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage; habitual SSB consumption at this sugar level is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines, and no cardioprotective nutrients are present in meaningful quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fruit juice drink with aloe vera is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink sweetened beverage made from fruit juice concentrate, water, added sweeteners, and suspended aloe vera pulp pieces. It is a clear to lightly opaque liquid with a mildly sweet, fruity flavour and occasional soft, gelatinous aloe vera pieces that provide a subtle textural contrast. The drink is ultra-processed and is categorised as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 8.99 g sugar per 100 g content. Per 100 g it provides 38 kcal, 8.18 g carbohydrate, 8.99 g sugar, 0.02 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 0.02 g dietary fibre, and 12 mg sodium. Calcium is present at 2 mg/100 g. The beverage is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a convenience snack or refreshment drink. Clinically, this drink is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high free-sugar content and negligible fibre. The aloe vera content in commercial formulations is typically too low to confer meaningful anti-inflammatory or glucose-modulating benefits. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low category, upper boundary) by analogy to similar fruit juice drinks in the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycemic load of approximately 11.2 per 250 ml serving. Healthier alternatives include plain water, unsweetened aloe vera water, or low-sugar beverages.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id D03070007), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for fruit juice drinks, appropriately documented. Local-language names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for a Singapore-catalogued commercial beverage.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — this is a water-based fruit beverage with no added fat by formulation. iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source, confirmed zero per HPB record. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 consistent with zero total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this product. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on fruit juice drink category data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific commercial product was found in approved sources. GI of 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney standard scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 20.45) / 100 = 11.2. Local-language names provided for Chinese (simplified: 芦荟果汁饮料; traditional: 蘆薈果汁飲料), Malay (Minuman jus buah dengan aloe vera), Tamil (அலோ வேரா பழச்சாறு பானம்), and Hindi (एलोवेरा फलों का जूस पेय) to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance, as this product is catalogued under the HPB Singapore database. All local-language alias strings appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with HPB nutrient data and University of Sydney GI analogous estimate.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Reviewed G6 compliance.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names for Chinese simplified (芦荟果汁饮料), Chinese traditional (蘆薈果汁飲料), Malay (Minuman jus buah dengan aloe vera), Tamil (அலோ வேரா பழச்சாறு பானம்), and Hindi (एलोवेरा फलों का जूस पेय) to local_names and aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 and Q1 and Q8. Added all local-language strings verbatim to flat aliases array to satisfy Q8 consistency rule. Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI of 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney standard classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), fixing Q4 failure.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
